title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
This interactive application helps users identify where urban growth in London is generating the highest environmental cost, and understand what types of land are being lost in the process.
It combines spatial exploration with borough-level comparison, allowing users to move from observing where growth has occurred to understanding the environmental trade-offs associated with that growth.
Open the interactive application
View the source code on GitHub






TipStart exploring



Use the interactive map to explore spatial patterns of urban growth and environmental cost.
Compare boroughs with the highest environmental cost using the ranking chart.
Examine the land-replacement chart to understand what types of environments are being lost.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
Project Summary

Problem Statement
Urban growth is often mapped in terms of where development occurs, but this rarely explains what is lost and what environmental costs are produced. New built-up areas may replace green space, encroach on water-sensitive zones, and increase local heat exposure.
This application addresses that gap by identifying where urban expansion has taken place across London, what land has been replaced, and whether these changes are associated with uneven environmental costs across boroughs.


End User
This application is designed for urban planners, local authorities, environmental teams, and community organisations. These users need accessible spatial evidence to understand whether recent urban growth has generated disproportionate environmental costs and where intervention may be needed.
The tool supports this by allowing users to identify high-cost growth areas, compare boroughs, and explore how development relates to green space loss, water-edge pressure, and heat exposure, without requiring specialist GIS expertise.


Data
The project uses multiple datasets available in Google Earth Engine. Dynamic World is used to detect land cover transitions and identify newly built-up areas. Sentinel-2 imagery is used to calculate NDVI as an indicator of vegetation change, while Landsat 8/9 is used to estimate land surface temperature and heat penalty.
JRC Global Surface Water is used to identify stable water bodies and potential encroachment on sensitive water-edge environments. London borough boundaries are used to aggregate results and enable comparison across the city.


Methodology
The analysis begins by identifying newly built-up areas through land cover change detection. For these expansion areas, a series of environmental indicators are calculated, including land replacement type, green space loss, NDVI reduction, heat penalty, and proximity to water edges.
These indicators are aggregated at borough level to enable comparison across London. A composite growth cost index is then constructed to summarise the environmental cost of urban expansion, highlighting spatial inequalities in development impact.


Interface
The interface combines an interactive map, control panel, and summary outputs to support a simple analytical workflow. Users can switch between layers showing new built-up areas, land replacement types, NDVI loss, heat penalty, and composite growth cost.
They can select a borough from a dropdown menu or click directly on the map to view local summaries. Supporting charts provide comparative insights, allowing users to move from spatial exploration to analytical understanding.

title: CASA00025 Group Project Title Here
text: Replace the link below with the link to your application.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text: The project combines two presentation layers. First, the Earth Engine application allows users to explore environmental indicators spatially through an interactive map interface. Second, the borough ranking chart summarizes borough-level outcomes in a form that supports rapid comparison.

Use this repository to host a website for your CASA0025 final project by following these stpes:

clone this repository
install quarto
edit the ‘index.qmd’ file with the contents of your project
using terminal, navigate to the project directory and run “quarto render”
push the changes to your github repository
on github, navigate to Settings>Pages>Build and Deployment. Make sure that under “Source” it says “deploy from branch”. Under “Branch”, select “Main” in the first dropdown and “Docs” under the second drop down. Then press “Save”

Your website should now be available under https://{your_username}.github.io/{your_repo_name}

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
This document describes all clean, analysis-ready outputs generated in B_preprocess_data.js for downstream visualization, interaction, and modeling.




All exported files are stored under this asset root path:

projects/london-growth-cost-explorer/assets/

In Earth Engine, each file is loaded by combining the root path with the asset name.


var ASSET_ROOT = 'projects/london-growth-cost-explorer/assets/';



var stack = ee.Image(ASSET_ROOT + 'vme50_london_growth_stack_c_and_d_ready');
var growthCost = ee.Image(ASSET_ROOT + 'vme50_london_growth_growth_cost_index');
var transition = ee.Image(ASSET_ROOT + 'vme50_london_growth_dw_transition_code');



var boroughSummary = ee.FeatureCollection(ASSET_ROOT + 'vme50_london_growth_borough_summary');
var rfTrain = ee.FeatureCollection(ASSET_ROOT + 'vme50_london_growth_rf_train_samples');
var rfTest = ee.FeatureCollection(ASSET_ROOT + 'vme50_london_growth_rf_test_samples');



Map.centerObject(boroughSummary, 9);
Map.addLayer(growthCost, {min: 0, max: 1, palette: ['#ffffcc', '#fd8d3c', '#800026']}, 'growth_cost_index');
Map.addLayer(transition, {min: 0, max: 88, palette: ['#f7fbff', '#6baed6', '#08306b']}, 'dw_transition_code', false);

Note: Table ID / Image ID shown in the Asset panel is the full asset path used directly in code.






The preprocessing workflow exports six files in total, grouped into four product types:

Raster stack for application layers and spatial analysis
Export name: vme50_london_growth_stack_c_and_d_ready
Derived single-band rasters for direct mapping and lightweight app loading
Export names:

vme50_london_growth_growth_cost_index

vme50_london_growth_dw_transition_code

Borough-level summary table (CSV)
Export name: vme50_london_growth_borough_summary
Random-forest-ready sample tables (CSV)
Export names:

vme50_london_growth_rf_train_samples

vme50_london_growth_rf_test_samples



Export tasks are executed only when B_CONFIG.DoExport = true.












Export file
Data type
Format
Main purpose




vme50_london_growth_stack_c_and_d_ready
Multi-band raster
GeoTIFF (image export)
Core analysis stack for map layers and downstream statistics


vme50_london_growth_growth_cost_index
Single-band raster
GeoTIFF (image export)
Direct visualization of integrated environmental cost hotspot intensity


vme50_london_growth_dw_transition_code
Single-band raster
GeoTIFF (image export)
Land-cover transition diagnostics (what changed to built-up)


vme50_london_growth_borough_summary
Vector table
CSV
Borough-level ranking, charting, and dashboard joins


vme50_london_growth_rf_train_samples
Sample table
CSV
Training dataset for Random Forest route


vme50_london_growth_rf_test_samples
Sample table
CSV
Independent testing dataset for Random Forest evaluation








The cAndDStack image is the main handoff product for mapping and analysis.
It includes the following bands:










Band name
Meaning
Unit
Typical value range
Primary use




new_built_2017_2025
Pixels that changed from non-built (2017) to built (2025)
binary
0/1
Core expansion mask; hotspot mapping


persisted_built
Pixels built in both 2017 and 2025
binary
0/1
Baseline built-up context


previous_dw_class
Dynamic World class in 2017 for new-built pixels
class ID
0-8 (masked outside new built)
Identify what land type was replaced


dw_transition_code
Transition code computed as 2017_class * 10 + 2025_class
integer code
e.g. 16 = trees->built
Transition diagnostics and legends


green_loss_to_built
New-built pixels that replaced green/open classes (trees, grass, crops, shrub)
binary
0/1
Green loss assessment


new_built_in_water_edge_zone
New-built pixels inside 100 m stable-water edge zone
binary
0/1
Water-edge pressure screening


NDVI_2017
Sentinel-2 NDVI baseline (2017 growing season composite)
NDVI index
approx. -1 to 1
Baseline vegetation condition


NDVI_2025
Sentinel-2 NDVI endpoint (2025 growing season composite)
NDVI index
approx. -1 to 1
Current vegetation condition


NDVI_delta_2025_minus_2017
NDVI change (2025 - 2017)
NDVI delta
approx. -1 to 1
Vegetation trend analysis


LST_2017_K
Landsat summer LST baseline (2017)
Kelvin
~285-315 K (context-dependent)
Thermal baseline


LST_2025_K
Landsat summer LST endpoint (2025)
Kelvin
~285-315 K (context-dependent)
Current thermal condition


LST_delta_K_2025_minus_2017
LST change (2025 - 2017)
Kelvin
typically negative/positive small values
Heat penalty diagnostics


growth_cost_index
Composite 0-1 expansion cost score on new-built pixels
normalized index
0-1 (masked outside new built)
Priority/ranking layer for policy focus







The workflow uses Google Dynamic World label IDs:



Class ID
Class name




0
water


1
trees


2
grass


3
flooded_vegetation


4
crops


5
shrub_and_scrub


6
built


7
bare


8
snow_and_ice







Each record corresponds to one London borough feature.









Field name
Meaning
Unit
Primary use




NAME / GSS_CODE
Borough name and code (from source boundary asset)
text
Join key in dashboards/charts


new_built_area_km2
Area converted to built between 2017-2025
km2
Expansion intensity comparison


green_loss_area_km2
Area of green/open classes replaced by new built
km2
Ecological opportunity-cost comparison


water_edge_pressure_area_km2
New built inside stable-water edge sensitivity zone
km2
Water-edge pressure comparison


mean_ndvi_loss_on_new_built
Mean positive NDVI loss over new-built pixels
NDVI units
Vegetation penalty indicator


mean_lst_penalty_k_on_new_built
Mean positive LST increase over new-built pixels
Kelvin
Thermal penalty indicator


mean_growth_cost_index
Mean composite cost score on new-built pixels
0-1 index
Borough ranking for intervention priority







The train/test CSV files are sampled from rfSamplingImage and are ready for model training.










Field name
Meaning
Unit / Type




label_rf
Binary class target (1 = new built, 0 = not new built)
integer


split_random
Random number used for train/test split
float [0,1)














Field name
Meaning
Unit




B2
Sentinel-2 blue reflectance (2025 composite)
unitless reflectance


B3
Sentinel-2 green reflectance (2025 composite)
unitless reflectance


B4
Sentinel-2 red reflectance (2025 composite)
unitless reflectance


B8
Sentinel-2 NIR reflectance (2025 composite)
unitless reflectance


NDVI_2017
Baseline NDVI
NDVI index


NDVI_2025
Endpoint NDVI
NDVI index


NDVI_delta_2025_minus_2017
NDVI change
NDVI delta


LST_C (from 2017 image)
Baseline LST converted from Kelvin to Celsius
deg C


LST_C_1 (from 2025 image)
Endpoint LST converted from Kelvin to Celsius
deg C


LST_delta_K_2025_minus_2017
LST change
Kelvin


water_occurrence
JRC long-term surface water occurrence
percent (0-100)


stable_water_buffer_100m_binary
Stable-water 100 m buffer flag
binary


dw_label_2017
Dynamic World class label in 2017
class ID (0-8)




Note: Earth Engine may append suffixes such as _1 when bands share names during concatenation (e.g., two LST_C bands from different years).







Visualization / storytelling: use new_built_2017_2025, green_loss_to_built, new_built_in_water_edge_zone, and growth_cost_index as core thematic layers.

Interaction / analytics: use boroughSummary for ranking panels and linked charts; use dw_transition_code for drill-down transition statistics.

Modeling track (Route A): use rf_train_samples and rf_test_samples directly for random forest baseline experiments and accuracy reporting.





All exported files use:

Prefix: vme50_london_growth_
Folder: GEE_Exports
Region: Greater London boundary

This consistent naming is designed for easy ingestion into app scripts, dashboards, and report pipelines.

Folder: GEE_Exports

This consistent naming is designed for easy ingestion into app scripts, dashboards, and report pipelines.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
The chart below shows the 10 boroughs with the highest mean environmental growth cost index.

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("data/vme50_london_growth_D_borough_ranking_clean.csv")

top10 = (
    df.sort_values("d_mean_growth_cost_index", ascending=False)
      .head(10)
      .sort_values("d_mean_growth_cost_index", ascending=True)
)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.barh(top10["NAME"], top10["d_mean_growth_cost_index"])
plt.xlabel("Mean Growth Cost Index")
plt.ylabel("Borough")
plt.title("Top 10 Boroughs by Environmental Growth Cost")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text: The ranking chart highlights substantial variation in environmental growth cost across London boroughs. Boroughs with higher scores are not necessarily those with the largest amount of new development, but rather those where new built-up land appears to have replaced ecologically valuable land or produced stronger local environmental penalties.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text: London’s recent urban growth does not only change where built-up land appears. It may also replace green space, intensify surface heat, reduce vegetation health, and increase pressure near sensitive water-edge environments. This project brings these environmental signals together in a single interactive application to help users identify where development may have generated the highest local environmental cost.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text: Many urban growth maps show where development happened, but not what was lost or what environmental penalties followed. This application moves beyond simple expansion mapping by highlighting ecological trade-offs and comparing how these costs vary across London boroughs.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
Interactive Map
Explore spatial patterns of urban growth and environmental cost across London.
Use the layer controls to switch between indicators such as newly built-up land, vegetation loss, heat exposure, and composite growth cost. Click a borough to inspect its local patterns.
Note: the map is hosted via Google Earth Engine and may occasionally take a few seconds to load depending on network or browser settings.
If the map does not load, click here to open the interactive map.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text: This application is designed for planners, local authorities, environmental teams, and community groups. It provides accessible spatial evidence for identifying where recent development may have produced higher environmental costs, without requiring specialist GIS knowledge.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text: The project uses borough-level outputs derived from Google Earth Engine, including growth cost index, green loss, NDVI loss, heat penalty, and water-edge encroachment. These indicators are based on land-cover transition analysis and environmental change metrics aggregated to borough level.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
Methodology
Data preprocessing and land-cover preparation established the base inputs for identifying newly built-up land and land-cover transitions across London.
Indicator construction produced the environmental cost measures used in the application, including vegetation loss, NDVI decline, heat penalty, water-edge encroachment, and a composite growth cost index.
Application development implemented the interactive Earth Engine interface, while visualisation and front-end integration translated the outputs into charts, explanatory text, and a project showcase page.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text: The interface combines two complementary presentation layers: an interactive Earth Engine map for spatial exploration and a borough ranking chart for summary comparison. This structure helps users move between local detail and city-wide overview.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text: This page presents the front-end showcase for the wider group project. The interactive Earth Engine application, indicator layers, and borough-level outputs were developed collaboratively across the team and integrated here as a public-facing project page.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
This interactive application helps users identify where urban growth in London is generating the highest environmental cost, and understand what types of land are being lost in the process.
It combines spatial exploration with borough-level comparison, allowing users to move from observing where growth has occurred to understanding the environmental trade-offs associated with that growth.
Open the interactive application
View the source code on GitHub






TipStart exploring



Use the interactive map to explore spatial patterns of urban growth and environmental cost.
Compare boroughs with the highest environmental cost using the ranking chart.
Examine the land-replacement chart to understand what types of environments are being lost.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
What This Project Reveals
Many urban growth maps show where development happened, but not what was lost or what environmental penalties followed. This project goes further by combining multiple environmental indicators into a single spatial story, helping users distinguish between growth that is merely extensive and growth that is environmentally costly.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
Explore the Map
Use the interactive application below to inspect newly built-up land, switch between environmental indicators, and compare borough-level patterns across London.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
section: Which Boroughs Show the Highest Cost?
text:
Which Boroughs Show the Highest Cost?
Hover over each bar to inspect exact borough-level values.
These rankings allow comparison of relative environmental cost across boroughs, rather than absolute levels of development.


                            
                                            








NoteKey Insight



Outer London boroughs such as Richmond upon Thames and Hounslow show the highest environmental growth cost.
This suggests that recent development in these areas is more likely to replace high-value green or natural land, rather than previously developed surfaces.
In contrast, inner boroughs tend to show lower cost, indicating that growth may be occurring on already urbanised land.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
What the Ranking Suggests
The ranking reveals substantial variation in environmental growth cost across London boroughs. Higher-cost boroughs are not simply those with more development. Instead, they are places where newly built-up land appears more strongly associated with ecological loss, vegetation decline, heat exposure, or water-edge pressure.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
How to Read the Application
Start with the map to investigate where growth took place and how different indicators vary across space. Then use the borough ranking to compare which boroughs show the highest overall environmental cost. Together, these two views connect local patterns with city-wide comparison.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
Behind the Application
This application is designed for planners, local authorities, environmental teams, and community groups who need accessible spatial evidence on where recent urban growth may be associated with higher environmental cost.
It uses Google Earth Engine data including land-cover transitions, NDVI, land surface temperature, and water-related indicators to support borough-level comparison and help users identify where further investigation or intervention may be needed.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
Urban growth is not only about expansion, but also about what is lost. This application reveals where recent development in London may have generated the greatest environmental cost.
Explore the interactive map

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
What Did New Development Replace?
This chart reveals which types of land are most frequently replaced by new built-up areas, highlighting the environmental trade-offs associated with urban expansion.
Hover over each segment to compare which land types were most frequently replaced in the boroughs with the largest total transition into built-up land.


                            
                                            








NoteKey Insight



Vegetation and water-related land types appear to be the most frequently replaced in high-growth boroughs.
This indicates that new development is often expanding into ecologically sensitive areas, such as green spaces and river-adjacent zones, rather than reusing previously built land.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
What the Results Suggest
Environmental cost is not evenly distributed across London.
Higher-cost boroughs are not simply those with more development, but those where growth is more strongly associated with vegetation loss, heat exposure, and ecological disruption.
The combined use of spatial layers and borough-level charts shows that urban expansion can produce very different environmental outcomes depending on where it occurs and what land it replaces.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
How to Use This Application
Start with the interactive map to explore where urban growth has taken place and how environmental cost varies across London.
Then use the borough ranking chart to identify which areas show the highest relative environmental cost. Finally, examine the land-replacement chart to understand what kinds of land are most frequently being converted into built-up surfaces.
Together, these components help users move from spatial observation to comparative interpretation.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
section: How the Cost Index is Calculated
text:
How the Cost Index is Calculated
The environmental growth cost index combines multiple indicators derived from Google Earth Engine:

vegetation loss and NDVI decline
heat exposure associated with new built-up land
pressure on water-adjacent land
land-cover transitions into built surfaces

Each indicator is normalised and aggregated to produce a composite score at borough level. This allows comparison across boroughs while capturing multiple dimensions of environmental impact.
This index is intended as a comparative diagnostic measure rather than a precise absolute measure of environmental damage.
The chart below complements the spatial patterns observed in the map by identifying which boroughs exhibit the highest overall environmental cost of urban growth.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
Limitations
This analysis relies on satellite-derived indicators which may not capture all fine-scale urban changes.
Uncertainty may arise from land-cover classification, temporal resolution, and the aggregation of multiple indicators into a single comparative index.
The growth cost index should therefore be interpreted as a diagnostic and comparative measure rather than an absolute representation of environmental impact.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
Why This Matters
By highlighting where urban growth is associated with ecological trade-offs, this application supports more informed planning decisions that prioritise not only development quantity but also environmental quality.
It helps shift attention from simply measuring how much development has occurred to understanding how and where that development may generate greater environmental cost.

title: London Growth Cost Explorer
text:
Team Contributions

A: data preprocessing and land-cover preparation
B: imagery processing and vegetation indicators
C: land transition analysis and supporting datasets
D: growth cost index construction and borough aggregation
E: Earth Engine application interface and interaction design
F: chart design, front-end integration, Quarto showcase page, and interface text
